summary:
Humans do not synthesize pantothenic acid; instead they rely on dietary intake for this essential nutrient despite being able to metabolize it further within cells. The absence of a functional pathway for de novo synthesis in human biology contradicts claims about internal production, making dietary absorption critical for maintaining adequate levels. 

Note: This summary focuses only on clinically relevant facts regarding metabolic pathways and nutritional requirements related directly to the query—no extraneous details are included.

The correct vitamin synthesized in vivo by humans does not exist among common vitamins because none can be produced internally due to lacking necessary enzymatic pathways. Pantothenic acid is often mistakenly believed to be synthesizable but actual data shows humans depend entirely on dietary sources without any known ability to produce it through metabolism.
